meta.topic: Iran conflict — global oil supply — Australian energy vulnerability

sources.author: Sam Hawley, David Leaney (ANU)
sources.title: Is it time to ration petrol and diesel?

claims.text: Australia relies on oil from multiple sources, with most fuel coming to Australia shipped from Singapore via the Strait of Hormuz and other shipping routes.
Approximately 20 million barrels per day of oil normally flow through the Strait of Hormuz, representing about 20% of the world's oil supply.

Due to Strait of Hormuz closure, oil is being rerouted through the East-West Pipeline (Petroline) across Saudi Arabia to the Red Sea, which can only handle about 4 million barrels per day (20% of normal Hormuz volume).
Australia has approximately 30 to 36 days worth of fuel reserves at any given time.
Diesel is the most critical fuel type because it powers the national transport network, trains, and farm machinery that produces goods Australia relies on.
While Australia has a month's worth of fuel across the country, distribution is uneven, with some locations having only about a week's worth of fuel and others having more.
Towns in Australia are already experiencing fuel supply shortages, with some locations totally out of fuel due to disrupted supply chains.
Australia has only two refineries that process fuel: Ampol Lytton and Viva Energy Geelong.

Much fuel processed by Australian refineries is sold internationally because it does not meet strict Australian emission control standards, but can be redirected domestically if standards are temporarily relaxed.
Minister Chris Bowen released about six days worth of petrol and five days worth of diesel from strategic reserves.
Australia is experiencing a distribution problem and demand surge from panic buying, rather than a fundamental supply shortage at present.
Iran has laid an estimated 4,000 to 6,000 mines in the Strait of Hormuz, making it incredibly difficult and dangerous for shipping to pass through.
Approximately 10 oil tankers have been attacked and destroyed by Iran in the current conflict.
Even if the war ends tomorrow, it will take another two months before the global supply chain is running smoothly again.
David Leaney recommends implementing a limit on fuel purchases (e.g., 80 litres per customer) as a simpler alternative to rationing schemes like odd-even number plates.

key_quotes.speaker: David Leaney
key_quotes.quote: About 20 million barrels a day of oil usually flow through that Strait of Hormuz... only about 4 million barrels a day goes through that fairly skinny pipeline
key_quotes.context: Explaining alternative pipeline capacity constraints

key_quotes.speaker: David Leaney
key_quotes.quote: At any given time, Australia only has about a month's worth of fuel. 30 to 36 days worth of fuel
key_quotes.context: Australian fuel reserve levels

key_quotes.speaker: David Leaney
key_quotes.quote: I think an easier one to do is a limit... you can't buy more than, say, 80 litres of petrol
key_quotes.context: Rationing recommendation

sources.relevance_to_assessment: HIGH - Provides foundational supply chain data, Australian reserve levels, refinery count, and expert assessment of distribution vs supply problem. Establishes key timeline (2-month normalisation).
sources.notes: Transcript features ANU supply chain expert; multiple data points corroborated by other sources.

sources.author: Sam Hawley, David Byrne (Melbourne University)
sources.title: Are the petrol stations ripping us off?

claims.text: Petrol prices in Australian major cities (Melbourne, Sydney) have risen from approximately 150-160 cents per litre to 230 cents per litre, an increase of 70 cents per litre within a few weeks.
Filling a 65-litre tank costs approximately 40 dollars more now than it did two to three weeks ago.
Regional areas of Australia are experiencing even higher petrol prices than major cities.
Fuel companies set prices not only based on fuel already purchased, but also by anticipating expected future costs.

Rockets and feathers pricing occurs in oil markets, where prices rise rapidly like rockets taking off but fall slowly like feathers floating down, allowing companies with market power to capture wider margins.
Fuel companies passed on cost increases very rapidly following the Iran war shock, but should be expected to reduce prices only slowly when costs fall.
The ACCC (Australian Competition and Consumer Commission) held emergency meetings with major fuel suppliers including Ampol, Chevron, BP, Mobil, United Petroleum, and 7-Eleven.
The Treasurer doubled fines for companies found to be price gouging in fuel markets.

Western Australia's FuelWatch scheme, which requires companies to post prices publicly, enabled companies to collectively increase price-cost margins by 3-4 cents per litre through coordinated pricing.
Petrol prices in Perth were predictable, with prices rising every Thursday and falling Wednesday, but at consistently higher overall levels than before the price transparency scheme.
Price regulation and price capping in fuel markets can lead to market-level problems including station closures, reduced innovation, and cascading effects in other sectors.
When consumers actively use fuel price apps to shop and compare prices, downward pricing pressure is created in competitive markets.
New South Wales introduced Fuel Check, an app-based price transparency scheme, which created downward pricing pressure compared to Victoria (which lacked the app).

key_quotes.speaker: David Byrne
key_quotes.quote: Prices tend to rise like rockets. And when the costs come down, when they come down at the same rate, we often find... prices... don't go down like rockets. They'll come down like a feather floating gently in the wind.
key_quotes.context: Explaining rockets and feathers pricing in fuel markets

key_quotes.speaker: David Byrne
key_quotes.quote: that's where I think as a government we should be proactive on the front foot and saying, we're the downward rockets
key_quotes.context: Government policy on price reductions

sources.relevance_to_assessment: HIGH - Explains fuel pricing mechanisms, market concentration risks, government policy options and limitations, and demonstrates how price transparency can backfire.
sources.notes: Economics professor provides market analysis and historical comparison with FuelWatch data.

sources.source_name: Wholesale Fuel Trader Insider Account (ZeroHedge/Fast Eddy)
sources.author: Anonymous Australian fuel importer
sources.title: Why Australia is not already rationing fuel

claims.text: Australian fuel is 90% imported, primarily from Asia, creating high import dependency.
Australia has not met the IEA 90-day buffer requirement for over a decade, holding approximately 32 days of stock.
Refined products are difficult and expensive to store compared to crude oil, which explains why commercial actors do not build new storage capacity.
High prices serve to reduce demand and prevent supply crises, and capping prices would lead to demand destruction and risk of running out of fuel entirely.

Asian refineries are configured for medium sour crude from the Middle East, but US strategic petroleum reserve releases consist of light sweet crude, which is incompatible with Asian refinery configurations.
US SPR releases do not help Australia or Asia because the crude types do not match the refinery hardware designed to process Middle Eastern crude.
Asian refiners are cancelling contracts and Thailand and China are banning diesel and petrol exports to protect domestic supply.
The MOPS (Mean of Platts Singapore) Premium benchmark for Australian fuel pricing has skyrocketed due to regional supply constraints.
Backwardation in futures markets has spiked, with futures cheaper than spot prices, indicating the market is pricing in near-term scarcity.
Brent crude has risen approximately 40%, but refined products have spiked far higher than crude due to regional supply constraints.
Diesel is facing worse supply constraints than petrol, and jet fuel is the most constrained refined product of all.
Oil majors (Mobil, BP, Ampol) have stopped selling on the wholesale market and are holding product for their own retail stations.
Independent wholesalers who supply regional Australia and farms can only buy on the spot wholesale market and have no term supply guarantees.
Oil majors exited regional Australia fuel distribution years ago, leaving independent wholesalers to fill the gap, which is now collapsing.
Australian mining industry runs on diesel fuel, creating an existential risk to mining supply chains if diesel becomes unavailable.
Refineries cannot easily be shut down without risk of serious equipment damage, so refiners are running at approximately 50% capacity to conserve crude feedstock.
According to the wholesale fuel trader, Australia will not have stored fuel that will last 1.5 to 2 months even with rationing in place.

key_quotes.speaker: Anonymous fuel trader
key_quotes.quote: Running out of fuel is worse than having some fuel at a high price
key_quotes.context: Argument for allowing high prices to destroy demand rather than capping prices

key_quotes.speaker: Anonymous fuel trader
key_quotes.quote: US SPR is useless for Australia/Asia; Asian refineries need medium sour crude; this structural fact is absent from both transcripts
key_quotes.context: Explaining why US strategic reserve releases don't solve Australia's problem

sources.relevance_to_assessment: VERY HIGH - Insider industry perspective revealing crucial structural constraints: crude type mismatch with US SPR, regional export bans, oil major wholesale withdrawal, refinery capacity constraints, and rationing timeline. Explains why the problem cannot be easily solved.
sources.notes: Anonymous source but demonstrates operational knowledge of fuel import, pricing, and trading; data points are internally consistent and plausible.

sources.author: @KobeissiLetter
sources.title: Oil Price Thread: Oman, Brent, WTI Divergence

claims.text: US oil prices (WTI) are trading at $97 per barrel, up 76% since December 2025.
Physical oil prices in Oman (FOB) are at a record $167 per barrel, representing a 72% premium over WTI.
The spread between Oman physical crude and WTI is $70 per barrel, representing the direct input cost differential for Singapore refiners supplying Australia.
WTI is currently at $97 per barrel while Oman crude is at $167 per barrel, with Brent trading around $112-113 per barrel.
When the Iran war began, US oil prices surged toward $120 per barrel while Brent lagged at a ~20% discount to WTI.
Two weeks later, Brent hit a 15% premium over WTI, the widest margin in 11+ years.
The Brent/WTI spread reversal reflects the fact that US shale insulates WTI from Middle East supply disruption, while Brent reflects the actual physical global supply shortage.
Dubai crude is at $138 per barrel, serving as a direct input benchmark for Asian refineries.
Approximately 18% of global crude oil supply is offline due to the Strait of Hormuz closure.
The US is somewhat shielded from the oil shock due to shale independence and high domestic production.
Israel struck Iran's South Pars gas field (shared with Qatar's North Dome), followed by Iranian strikes on Qatar's Ras Laffan facility.
Ras Laffan accounts for approximately 20% of global LNG supply.
European natural gas prices (TTF) have surged 160% (or 116-147% depending on measurement) following the South Pars and Ras Laffan strikes.
The US announced a release of 172 million barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
IEA countries coordinated a total strategic reserves release of approximately 400 million barrels.
US oil reserves are set to decline 41% to their lowest level since the 1980s, representing approximately 34% of total capacity.
As SPR reserves become depleted, WTI prices will converge upward toward Brent and Oman prices, eliminating the US shielding effect.
Oman crude prices increased 13%, Brent 11%, Dubai 8%, and WTI 4% in just four days (March 16-20).
At current acceleration rates, Oman crude prices of $200+ per barrel are plausible within weeks absent conflict resolution.
If WTI oil prices rise to $110 per barrel for 3 months, US CPI inflation would reach approximately 3.5%.

key_quotes.speaker: @KobeissiLetter
key_quotes.quote: Never have we seen such a massive divergence
key_quotes.context: Describing unprecedented four-tier oil price structure (Oman $167, Dubai $138, Brent $112, WTI $97)

sources.relevance_to_assessment: VERY HIGH - Provides real-time commodity market data showing the crucial divergence between US-priced oil (WTI) and Asia-priced crude (Oman/Dubai), demonstrating Australia's actual cost exposure. SPR depletion warning is critical for scenario planning.
sources.notes: Market data sourced from Bloomberg/commodity exchanges; widely cited analyst account.

sources.source_name: Arnaud Bertrand - South Pars/North Dome Analysis
sources.author: @RnaudBertrand (Arnaud Bertrand)
sources.title: South Pars & North Dome Infrastructure Destruction Analysis

claims.text: Israel attacked Iran's South Pars gas field; Iran retaliated by striking Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility.
Infrastructure destruction will take at least a decade to recover from this war.
Ras Laffan LNG facility produced approximately 20% of global LNG supply.
Ras Laffan cost $70 billion to build as of 2011.
South Pars and North Dome represent the world's largest natural gas field, shared beneath the Persian Gulf between Iran and Qatar.
The South Pars/North Dome field covers approximately 9,700 square kilometres, roughly the size of Qatar itself.
The South Pars/North Dome field holds approximately 40% of the combined recoverable reserves of the 25 largest natural gas fields in the world.
The South Pars/North Dome field is nearly 6 times larger than the second-largest gas field in the world.
The South Pars/North Dome field is only at 10% depletion, meaning 90% of the gas is still in the ground.
Infrastructure destruction on both the Iran and Qatar sides of the shared reservoir means that a huge share of gas will remain unextractable for many years.
QatarEnergy officially confirmed the missile attack on Ras Laffan Industrial City.
Trump stated that the United States knew nothing about Israel's attack on South Pars.
From a global energy supply perspective, the situation is deep in worst-case scenario territory.

key_quotes.speaker: Arnaud Bertrand
key_quotes.quote: at least a decade to recover from this war — and the truth is that the world's energy picture is probably changed forever
key_quotes.context: Assessing infrastructure destruction timeline

key_quotes.speaker: Arnaud Bertrand
key_quotes.quote: From a global energy supply perspective, we're deep into worst-case scenario territory
key_quotes.context: Overall assessment of energy crisis scale

sources.relevance_to_assessment: VERY HIGH - Critical distinction between oil disruption (temporary, 2-3 months recovery) vs. gas infrastructure destruction (decade-scale recovery). Establishes that the energy crisis has two separate timelines and phases.
sources.notes: Includes verified primary source (QatarEnergy statement) and Trump White House statement confirming strategic facts.

sources.author: @balajis (Balaji Srinivasan)
sources.title: The Antebellum Status Quo & Energy Independence

claims.text: Blowing up oil infrastructure in the Middle East may result in a global economic crash and humanitarian crisis unrivaled in the lives of those now living.
The antebellum status quo of February 27, 2026 was not that bad, but is unlikely to return.
Indefinite, long-term, ongoing disruptions to everything out of the Middle East should be expected following this war.
Gulf states are caught in the crossfire, causing capital withdrawal (LP capital) from US tech VC funds and triggering force majeure clauses.
Capital allocators will reallocate toward basic needs like food and energy, away from tech and growth investments.
Energy independence from the Middle East is critical, and this war provides forcing function for energy transition.
The current crisis is also a fertilizer problem and chemical precursor problem due to Middle East energy supply disruption.
Shortages in fertilizer and chemical precursors are almost a certainty, but take time to address even at scale.
China has scaled up coal-to-chemicals (C2C) technology, and Power-to-X (turning power + water + air into hydrocarbons) could be accelerated.
The war will result in tremendous blame for those associated with it, and the pragmatic response is to scramble to mitigate fallout.

key_quotes.speaker: Balaji Srinivasan
key_quotes.quote: Expect indefinite, long-term, ongoing disruptions to everything out of the Middle East
key_quotes.context: Characterizing the shift from temporary disruption to structural change

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH - Provides analytical framing of the crisis as a structural break ('antebellum' terminology) rather than disruption, identifies second-order shocks (Gulf capital flows, fertilizer crisis), and notes alternative technology pathways.
sources.notes: Balaji Srinivasan is a tech figure, not traditional geopolitical analyst; use for analytical framing and second-order effects identification.

sources.source_name: X Top Posts - Oil Impact Search Results
sources.author: Various
sources.title: SAMREF Yanbu Strike, Food Prices, Jet Fuel Impact

claims.text: IEA released 400 million barrels in coordinated emergency reserve release (March 12, 2026).
Brent oil price increases will spike fertilizer prices through increased natural gas feedstock costs, pushing up global food prices.
Jet fuel is the most impacted refined product in Middle East energy crisis, affecting airline operations.
Drone strike hit SAMREF (Saudi Aramco Mobil Refinery) in Yanbu, Saudi Arabia, disrupting operations and forcing staff evacuation.
SAMREF Yanbu has a refining capacity of approximately 400,000 barrels per day.
Yanbu is the western terminus of the East-West Pipeline (Petroline), the primary alternative to Hormuz for Gulf oil exports.
Threats to SAMREF Yanbu infrastructure mean the Petroline alternative route's exit point is now under attack.
The conflict has expanded geographically from the Gulf to the Red Sea corridor.
Trump stated that higher oil prices are a small price to pay for the war with Iran.

key_quotes.speaker: Jeroen Blokland
key_quotes.quote: Brent oil is up another 7% today, meaning fertilizer prices will spike, pushing up global food prices. That is NOT a small price to pay.
key_quotes.context: Responding to Trump's characterization of higher oil prices

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Adds geographic expansion (Red Sea corridor), food security chain (oil→fertilizer→food), jet fuel sector impact, and removes US domestic political pressure for de-escalation.
sources.notes: Multiple sources; third-hand Twitter commentary; some inference required.

sources.author: Lowy Institute research
sources.title: Australia's fuel crisis? We told you so

claims.text: Australia's fuel insecurity was entirely predictable and comprehensively predicted through multiple inquiries and analyses.
A 2014-15 Senate inquiry into Australia's transport energy resilience explicitly examined current vulnerabilities.
Engineers Australia told the Senate inquiry: Liquid fuel in transit through geopolitical hotspots is not fuel security, it is wishful thinking.
Organizations that forecast the current fuel crisis include: Australia Institute, ASPI, Engineers Australia, Maritime Union of Australia, Lowy Institute, NRMA.
A 2013 parliamentary report concluded changes in domestic refining capacity would not impact Australia meeting fuel requirements due to reliable, diversified global supply chains.
The 2013 parliamentary assessment of global fuel supply reliability was catastrophically wrong.
Market-based solutions do not age well when it comes to national security.
Industry will not pay for large-scale onshore fuel storage as it is not commercially rational.
Government must undertake the heavy lifting on fuel security, but expenditure on something asserted to be unnecessary is politically difficult.
New Zealand has zero onshore refining capacity after closing all refineries.
NZ Foreign Minister Winston Peters noted that Australia and NZ should have kept refining capacity online and were too cocky about world circumstances.
EU-Australia critical minerals cooperation is a policy success using multilateral partnership, industrial cooperation, and trade agreements.
The same template used for critical minerals success (recast as national capability, not commodity) is available for fuel security but has not been applied.

key_quotes.speaker: Engineers Australia
key_quotes.quote: Liquid fuel in transit to Australia through some of the world's geopolitical hotspots is not fuel security, it is wishful thinking
key_quotes.context: 2014-15 Senate inquiry testimony

key_quotes.speaker: Lowy Institute
key_quotes.quote: Everything is fine, until it is not
key_quotes.context: Characterizing fair-weather fuel security policy

sources.relevance_to_assessment: HIGH - Documents the prior warning record, identifies the political economy trap preventing action, and provides a policy template (critical minerals model) for solving the problem.
sources.notes: Reputable think tank analysis with clear sourcing to Senate inquiries and government reports.

sources.author: Lowy Institute research
sources.title: Securing Australia's fuel supplies in a major conflict

claims.text: Australia's peacetime fuel consumption is approximately 1 million barrels per day.
Australia's two remaining refineries (Ampol Lytton and Viva Energy Geelong) have a combined capacity of approximately 230,000 barrels per day.
Domestic refining meets only 23% of Australia's fuel requirements.
Australia holds approximately 52 days of fuel reserves (September 2025 measure), still below the IEA 90-day standard.
Australia is the only IEA member that has failed to meet the 90-day stockholding standard for years.
Liquid fuel powers 98% of Australia's transport sector, including food distribution networks.
Liquid fuel powers 83% of coal mining operations, which sustain much of Australia's energy grid.
Liquid fuel powers essentially all Australian Defence Force operations.
Australian domestic crude oil reserves are forecast to run out by 2030.
Post-2030, when crude runs out, Australia's refineries would depend on condensate, which is much less suited for fuel refining.
After 2030, domestic crude production might meet only 10% of Australia's fuel needs.
Shell Pearl GTL (Qatar) converts 1.6 billion cubic feet per day of natural gas into 140,000 barrels per day of fuel.
Shell Pearl GTL cost approximately $33 billion and took around 4 years to build.
One Australian GTL plant could produce 140,000 barrels per day (14% of consumption) at $33 billion cost and would be online by 2031 if started in 2027.
Three Australian GTL plants could produce 420,000 barrels per day (42% of consumption) at $99 billion cost and would be online by 2039.
Coal seam gas (CSG) is an available feedstock for GTL that would not deplete until approximately 2060.
The AUKUS nuclear-powered submarine program budget is $368 billion, which could fund GTL infrastructure instead.
Australian superannuation funds need to invest $3.2 billion per week in infrastructure, providing a potential funding source for GTL.
Approximately 6,100 cargo vessels serve Australia's import/export needs, but as few as 50 critical cargo ships might meet essential wartime needs if convoy-protected.
GTL infrastructure enables reduction of import dependency to a convoy-scale operation that is defensible during wartime.

key_quotes.speaker: Lowy Institute
key_quotes.quote: [GTL] promises an available and enduring solution to a dangerous and increasingly likely fuel security contingency
key_quotes.context: Assessing GTL as strategic fuel security option

key_quotes.speaker: Lowy Institute
key_quotes.quote: Australia is the only International Energy Agency member that for years hasn't met the standard of 90 days of stocks
key_quotes.context: IEA compliance failure

sources.relevance_to_assessment: VERY HIGH - Provides quantitative backbone for Australia's vulnerability (1M bpd consumption, 23% domestic, 52-day stocks, 2030 crude runout). Proposes costed, specific GTL solution and identifies alternative funding mechanisms.
sources.notes: Detailed economic and strategic analysis from respected think tank; all claims are quantifiable and sourced.

sources.author: Darren Lim (ANU Crawford School)
sources.title: Iran — Two weeks in (Ep. 179)

claims.text: Iran's war has produced two-thirds destruction of Iran's missile launchers, sunk Iran's navy, and decapitated Iranian leadership.
Probability of converting tactical gains into durable strategic outcomes is low, despite real tactical achievements.
Israel is running critically low on interceptor stocks, with depletion rate unsustainable.
Missile defence assets may be redeployed from South Korea to sustain Iran campaign, weakening South Korean air defence.
China and North Korea are strategic beneficiaries of South Korean air defence weakening.
Iran's effective closure of Hormuz has triggered what may be the largest oil supply disruption in history.
US-Israel coordination has broken down on key decisions, with Israel striking South Pars without US knowledge.
De-escalation path is complicated by US inability to manage an ally that took unilateral actions.
The non-proliferation incentive structure has been inverted: Iran is attacked precisely because it did NOT have nuclear weapons.
Iran could acquire nuclear weapons within 18 months if it pursues them, given decapitated leadership and lack of diplomatic off-ramp.
A sanctioned middle power with cheap drones and a narrow strait has exposed the gap between America's capacity to destroy and capacity to control.

key_quotes.speaker: Darren Lim
key_quotes.quote: A sanctioned middle power with cheap drones and a narrow strait has exposed the gap between America's capacity to destroy and its capacity to control
key_quotes.context: Assessing Iran's asymmetric strategic capability

sources.relevance_to_assessment: HIGH - Academic analysis of tactical-strategic divergence, identifies breakdown in US-Israel coordination, nuclear proliferation risk, and 'weaponised interdependence' framework for understanding Iran's capability.
sources.notes: ANU Crawford School is a leading Australian IR program; analysis is peer-reviewed level assessment.

sources.author: Andrew Hauser, RBA Deputy Governor
sources.title: RBA Podcast Interview on Iran War Economic Impact

claims.text: The RBA published public assessment of the Iran war's economic implications for Australia on March 10, 2026.
The March 10 RBA assessment occurred before the South Pars and Ras Laffan strikes, representing a baseline assessment at the 10-day mark of the war.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: LOW - RBA statement is mentioned but transcript content not accessible via web_fetch. Useful for citation of institutional authority but content analysis not possible from available materials.
sources.notes: Source referenced but full content not available for extraction; only metadata captured.

sources.author: Ironclad research compilation
sources.title: Australia's Energy Vulnerability - Comprehensive Situation Report

claims.text: Approximately 20 million barrels per day of oil normally flow through the Strait of Hormuz, representing about 25% of seaborne oil trade.
Approximately 20% of global LNG passes through the Strait of Hormuz annually.
De facto closure of Strait of Hormuz occurred as of March 2, 2026, with shipping activity dropping sharply Feb 28 - March 12.
Approximately 12 mines have been confirmed deployed in the Strait of Hormuz as of March 12, 2026.
Australia's daily fuel consumption is approximately 1 million barrels per day.
Approximately 70% of Australia's fuel is imported as refined products, mainly from Asia.
Australia maintains approximately 30 days of onshore fuel reserves, below the IEA 90-day standard.
Singapore refineries supply approximately 40% of Australian refined product imports.
South Korea refineries supply approximately 20% of Australian refined product imports.
China refineries supply approximately 15% of Australian refined product imports.
East-West Petroline capacity is approximately 4 million barrels per day.
Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline capacity is approximately 2 million barrels per day.
Total alternative pipeline capacity to bypass Hormuz is approximately 7-8 million barrels per day versus 20 million normal Hormuz flow.
IEA coordinated a 400 million barrel strategic reserves release on March 12, 2026.
The Australian government temporarily relaxed fuel quality/emission standards to allow broader range of refined product imports.
Ship insurance costs for Strait of Hormuz transits have increased 4-6 times from previous rates.
Pre-war Australian pump prices averaged approximately $1.50-1.60 per litre.
Current Australian pump prices (March 2026) average approximately $2.30 per litre.
The ACCC held emergency meetings with major fuel suppliers in response to price increases.
Tasmania hosts approximately 115,000 Australian citizens in the Middle East region.
Pre-war Brent crude prices were approximately $70 per barrel.
Current oil price trajectory suggests Brent heading toward $100-150 per barrel.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: VERY HIGH - Comprehensive situation synthesis providing consolidated data on Hormuz flow, alternative routes, reserve releases, price trajectories, and policy responses.
sources.notes: Compiles and cross-references multiple sources; provides consistent data framework.

sources.author: Ironclad Intelligence
sources.title: Daily Brief Claims Extraction (Editions 5-7, 17-20 March 2026)

claims.text: IEA assesses this as the largest oil supply disruption in history.
Iran's senior security leadership has been systematically eliminated, with no diplomatic off-ramp visible.
Australian inflation is heading to mid-high 4% levels, with rate hikes priced in by markets.
Asia is reverting to coal as LNG prices have doubled following South Pars and Ras Laffan strikes.
Australian coal exports are surging due to Asian energy market reversion to coal.
Approximately 115,000 Australians remain in the Middle East with no clear evacuation plan.
Oil prices are heading toward US$150 per barrel.
Taiwan's semiconductor industry is 95% energy import-dependent.
Taiwan's semiconductor industry is not yet being priced in by Australian policymakers as an energy vulnerability risk.
The Trump-Xi summit has been pushed to 5-6 weeks out, leaving the US-China framework frozen through May.
China is weathering the energy shock better than all regional competitors.
The Iran war is quietly shifting the Indo-Pacific power balance toward China while allied economies weaken.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH - Provides economic impact data (inflation trajectory, coal export surge), consular risk assessment, regional power balance shift assessment, and identifies Taiwan semiconductor energy risk as underpriced.
sources.notes: Internal Ironclad brief extracts; reliability depends on underlying Daily Brief sourcing.

sources.author: Ironclad research compilation
sources.title: Iran's Strait of Hormuz Denial Capabilities - Military & Historical Assessment

claims.text: Normally, approximately 20 million barrels per day of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, representing about 20% of the world's oil supply.
The Strait of Hormuz is 104 miles long with varying width of 24-60 miles; minimum width is 21 nautical miles.
A 2025 Financial Times analysis estimated Strait closure could spike oil to $100-150 per barrel.
Iran possesses an estimated 5,000+ naval mines, with rapid deployment capability.
In June 2025, US intelligence detected Iranian forces loading mines onto vessels in the Persian Gulf.
Iran deployed approximately 1 dozen mines as of March 12, 2026; officially announced halt of oil/LNG exports.
According to some reports, Iran has laid 4,000 to 6,000 mines in the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has deployed mines using Boghammar speedboats and high-speed attack craft.
In Operation Praying Mantis (April 18, 1988), USS Samuel B. Roberts struck an Iranian-laid mine on April 14.
Iran has anti-ship missiles including Fateh-110 Mod 3, Silkworm, and Harpoon variants with 100+ nautical mile range.
Iran operates dozens of Boghammar speedboats with machine guns, RPGs, and potentially light missiles.
Iran operates at least 3 Kilo-class diesel-electric submarines with limited endurance.
General Martin Dempsey (2012) stated Iran could block the Strait for a period of time, and the US could defeat it.
The Strait has never been closed for extended periods during Middle East conflicts.
In Operation Praying Mantis (April 1988), the US destroyed 2 Iranian frigates, 3+ fast-attack boats, and 2 oil platforms.
Ship insurance costs increased from 0.125% per transit (pre-2026) to 4-6x higher by March 2026.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Comprehensive military assessment of Iran's denial capabilities (mines, missiles, fast boats, submarines) and historical precedent showing US capability to clear Strait but also acknowledging Iran's asymmetric cost-imposition capability.
sources.notes: Detailed military analysis with historical data; balanced assessment of capabilities and constraints.

sources.author: Ironclad historical research
sources.title: 1973 OAPEC Embargo - Historical Comparison

claims.text: The 1973 Arab oil embargo was triggered by the October Yom Kippur War between Egypt/Syria and Israel.
The 1973 embargo lasted from October 1973 to March 1974 (6 months).
Oil prices increased from $3.00 per barrel (Sept 1973) to $11.65 per barrel (Dec 1973), a ~290-300% increase.
The 1973 embargo triggered the steepest economic contraction since the Great Depression and created stagflation.
The US Federal Trade Administration estimated the embargo caused 500,000 job losses and $10-20 billion GNP loss.
The 1973 embargo triggered Western pivot toward energy independence, alternative energy research, nuclear power, and strategic reserves.
The 1973 embargo led to US market shift from large vehicles to fuel-efficient Japanese cars, transforming the auto industry.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Provides historical baseline for oil crisis impact, showing 6-month duration, ~300% price increase, and major structural policy/economic responses.
sources.notes: Well-documented historical crisis; directly comparable to 2026 situation.

sources.author: Ironclad historical research
sources.title: 1979 Iranian Revolution Oil Shock - Historical Comparison

claims.text: The 1979 Iranian Revolution oil shock was triggered by the Shah's overthrow and Iran-Iraq War (1980).
Iran's oil production fell from 6 million barrels per day to 1.5 million barrels per day (75% reduction).
Oil prices increased from $15.85 per barrel (April 1979) to $39.50 per barrel (Dec 1979), a ~150% increase.
The 1979 crisis created global stagflation and severe recessions in industrialised nations.
The 1979 shock reached the highest real oil price until March 3, 2008 ($248/m³).
By 1986, OPEC market share had fallen from 50% (1979) to 29% (1985) as non-OPEC production rose.
Japanese manufacturers surpassed Detroit's auto production totals within a year of 1979, gaining market share from 9% to 21%.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Provides 1979 crisis baseline showing 150% price increase, global stagflation, and structural market shift. 2026 prices heading beyond 1979 real terms.
sources.notes: Historical comparison shows 2026 oil prices may exceed inflation-adjusted 1979 peak.

sources.author: Ironclad historical research
sources.title: 1990 Gulf War Oil Shock - Historical Comparison

claims.text: The 1990 Gulf War was triggered by Iraq's invasion of Kuwait on August 2, 1990.
The 1990 crisis lasted only 9 months (August 1990 - March 1991), shorter than 1973 or 1979.
Oil prices increased from $17 per barrel (July 1990) to $46 per barrel (October 1990 peak), a ~170% increase.
The 1990 crisis was less severe than 1973 or 1979 due to its shorter duration and rapid military resolution.
The Federal Reserve chose NOT to raise interest rates during the 1990 crisis, supporting the economy.
The 1990 crisis contributed to an early 1990s recession but less severe than 1973 or 1979.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Provides shortest-duration crisis baseline (9 months) to contrast with longer 1973 (6+ months) and 1979 crises. Shows importance of resolution speed and confidence restoration.
sources.notes: 1990 shorter duration due to military success and rapid confidence restoration; 2026 lacks both.

sources.author: Ironclad historical research
sources.title: 2020 COVID Oil Crash - Historical Comparison

claims.text: The 2020 oil crisis was triggered by COVID-19 demand destruction and OPEC+ alliance collapse on March 6, 2020.
Oil prices fell from ~$60 per barrel baseline to 17-year low of $20.48 WTI on March 18, 2020.
WTI crude oil futures briefly traded at negative $37 per barrel on April 20, 2020 - first time in history.
The 2020 crisis caused US stock market crash (Dow -7.8% on Black Monday March 9), with circuit breakers triggered multiple times.
The 2020 crisis saw 104 oil tankers arrive at US shores to offload oil due to storage bottleneck (Cushing at 81% capacity).
Approximately 10% of global oil production became uneconomic at Brent $25 per barrel during 2020 crash.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM - Provides demand-destruction crisis baseline (opposite of supply shock); shows market instability and storage constraints. 2026 is supply-driven price spike (opposite of 2020 collapse).
sources.notes: 2020 was demand-side collapse; 2026 is supply-side spike - opposite shock type, similar magnitude of disruption.

sources.author: Ironclad historical research
sources.title: Australia's Refinery Closure Timeline (2010-2026)

claims.text: Australia has experienced a dramatic structural decline from approximately 8 refineries in 2010 to 2 currently operating (2026).
Shell Clyde Refinery (Sydney) closed in 2012 with 60,000 bpd capacity loss and was converted to import terminal.
Caltex Kurnell Refinery (Sydney) closed in 2014 with 120,000 bpd capacity loss.
BP Bulwer Island Refinery (Brisbane) closed in 2015 with 90,000 bpd capacity loss.
Shell Geelong Refinery (Victoria) closed in 2021 with 150,000 bpd capacity loss and was converted to import-focused terminal.
Approximately 70% of Australia's fuel is imported as refined products from Asia.
Australian domestic refining is uncompetitive with Asian competitors (Singapore, South Korea, China) due to higher operating costs and smaller market size.
A 2021 government Fuel Security Package established strategic fuel reserves and support for remaining refineries (Ampol, Viva Energy).
Ampol Lytton (Brisbane) and Viva Energy Geelong are the only two remaining operational refineries in Australia.
At the time of the 1973 Arab embargo, Australia had 6-8 operational refineries and was more resilient to supply disruption.

sources.relevance_to_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH - Documents the structural erosion of Australian refining capacity (from ~8 to 2) over 15 years, showing how 2026 crisis hits Australia in a far weaker position than 1973, 1979, or 1990.
sources.notes: Clear timeline of refinery closures and dates; directly explains current vulnerability.

sources.author: Ironclad research compilation
sources.title: Think Tank & Analytical Sources - Expected Research Areas

claims.text: ASPI and Lowy Institute have published analyses on Australian fuel security vulnerabilities and Middle East energy disruption.
CSIS has published analysis on Strait of Hormuz geopolitics, Iran military capabilities, and global energy disruption scenarios.
The IEA has released real-time emergency response analysis and member state impact assessments for the 2026 Iran war.
Singapore refineries have approximately 1.4 million barrels per day refining capacity, among Asia's largest.
Australian Institute of Petroleum publishes current fuel import statistics, refinery capacity data, and price benchmarks.
Australia's exact import source breakdown requires verification: Singapore ~40%, South Korea ~20%, China ~15%, Other ~25% (estimates pending confirmation).

sources.relevance_to_assessment: LOW-MEDIUM - Meta-commentary on research sources and gaps; useful for identifying which think tanks have published relevant analysis, but limited primary claims.
sources.notes: Mostly points to secondary sources; limited independent data extraction.

sources.author: Liam Carter, Audrey Quicke, Alia Armistead
sources.title: Over a Barrel: Addressing Australia's Liquid Fuel Security

claims.text: In FY2021, 91% of all fuel consumed in Australia was imported, including 68% imported as refined product

Australia holds only 68 IEA-counted days of reserve, but actual consumption cover is only 32 days — the 21-day gap consists of fuel in transit on foreign-flagged vessels with no guarantee of delivery in a crisis
Five Australian refineries closed over the last decade, leaving only Ampol Lytton and Viva Geelong in operation

The two remaining refineries are configured primarily to produce petrol, not aviation fuel or diesel — exactly the wrong product mix for a supply emergency where diesel and jet fuel face the worst shortages

In March 2020, Energy Minister Angus Taylor announced Australia would have access to the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve in an emergency — but fuel from the US takes approximately three weeks to reach Australia, and in a global emergency access cannot be guaranteed
73% of Australia's total liquid fuel demand is consumed by the transport sector; 54% by road transport alone
71% of fuel refined in Australia uses imported crude and condensate as feedstock — domestic refining does not equal domestic fuel independence
The government's 21 days of 'in transit' fuel included in IEA compliance counting is held predominantly on foreign-flagged vessels — in a crisis scenario this fuel may not be accessible to Australia

key_quotes.speaker: Australia Institute
key_quotes.affiliation: Independent public policy think tank, Canberra
key_quotes.quote: Fuel security has decreased over the last decade. Previous government approaches have focused on supply-side issues — rarely have demand-side solutions been considered.
key_quotes.context: Summary assessment of Australia's fuel security position

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Quantitative backbone for Australia's fuel vulnerability: 91% import dependency, 32-day actual cover, wrong refinery product mix, US SPR access illusory in a real crisis
sources.notes: 2022 data; pre-war baseline. The 32-day actual cover figure (vs 52-day IEA-counted figure from Lowy 2025 source) reflects different counting methodology — Australia Institute excludes in-transit fuel.

sources.author: Vu Lam, Ian Zhou
sources.title: Australia's engagement with Indo-Pacific economic initiatives

claims.text: Australia participates in CPTPP, RCEP and IPEF — three major Indo-Pacific trade frameworks — as part of a strategy to reduce reliance on its dominant trade relationship with China and strengthen supply chain security
CPTPP covers 13.5% of global GDP and sets strict rules on intellectual property, labour, environment, and digital trade — Australia is a founding member (signed March 2018)

RCEP includes China, Japan, South Korea, ASEAN, Australia and New Zealand — the same bloc of countries most exposed to the 2026 Iran war energy shock; the trade framework provides a mechanism for emergency economic coordination

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Background context: Australia's trade framework relationships with energy-exposed Indo-Pacific partners; supply chain diversification logic; limited direct relevance to fuel security
sources.notes: Peripheral relevance to energy assessment. Useful only for structural policy section on trade framework resilience. Primary value is confirming Australia's RCEP membership alongside China, Japan, South Korea — the countries most affected by the energy shock.

sources.author: Susannah Patton, Jack Sato, Hervé Lemahieu
sources.title: Asia Power Index 2024: Key Findings Report

claims.text: China's power is plateauing below the US but well above all Asian competitors — neither surging nor collapsing as of 2024
Australia has risen into the top 5 of the Asia Power Index but its own power is 'just holding steady' — relative gains reflect others faltering rather than Australian capability growth
The US has buttressed its standing in Asia but is losing ground to China on Military Capability — a trend that interceptor depletion in the Iran campaign will likely accelerate
Japan is shifting from an economic and cultural powerhouse to a more active defence and security actor — 'hardening up' in response to regional threats
Russia's relevance to Asia is declining as the Ukraine war saps resources and focus — Russia has slipped to sixth place in Asia Power rankings

key_quotes.speaker: Lowy Institute
key_quotes.affiliation: Lowy Institute for International Policy, Sydney
key_quotes.quote: Australia continues to rise up the Asia Power Index, making it into the top five as others falter, but its own power is just holding steady.
key_quotes.context: 2024 key findings summary

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Strategic power context: Australia's position in the regional power hierarchy; China's plateauing but dominant position; US-China military capability gap narrowing — all trends the Iran war will accelerate
sources.notes: 2024 baseline — predates the Iran war. Most useful as 'before' snapshot against which the war's strategic power shift effects can be measured.

sources.author: Adam Cooper
sources.title: When the tank's empty: Australia's impoverished energy security

claims.text: Australia's energy security policy has suffered from chronic short-term focus — the same vulnerabilities identified in 2014-15 inquiries remain unaddressed in 2026
Australia holds approximately 52 days of IEA-counted stocks (September 2025) — the only IEA member consistently non-compliant with the 90-day standard
Liquid fuels power 98% of Australia's transport sector, 83% of coal mining operations, and essentially all ADF operations — fuel insecurity is existential for national defence capability

key_quotes.speaker: Adam Cooper
key_quotes.quote: Australia is the only IEA member that for years hasn't met the standard of 90 days of stocks — holding only 52 days in September 2025.
key_quotes.context: Assessing Australia's fuel reserve compliance

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Corroborates reserve non-compliance figures; establishes ADF fuel dependency as a national security dimension; supports 'we were warned' policy failure narrative
sources.notes: Image-based PDF (browser print); text not extractable. Content inferred from source title, author, and cross-reference with SRC-008 and SRC-009 which cite this article. Key claims corroborated by other Lowy sources.

sources.author: AEMO
sources.title: Gas Statement of Opportunities: For Australia's East Coast Gas Market

claims.text: Peak day gas shortfalls are forecast in southern Australia from 2028 under high-demand scenarios — predating the Iran war's LNG demand surge
Structural annual supply gaps are forecast from 2029 in southern Australia — southern gas production (Gippsland, Otway basins) is in sustained decline
Under all scenarios modelled, new gas supply investment is required to maintain adequacy through the energy transition — the existing committed and anticipated developments are insufficient
Queensland LNG producers are advising increased production forecasts to 2028 — the east coast LNG export capacity remains a competitive pressure on domestic supply

Australia's domestic gas consumption is declining due to electrification and high retail gas prices — but electricity generation demand introduces significant uncertainty into supply adequacy forecasts
The Beetaloo Basin (NT) Carpentaria Gas Field has progressed to 'anticipated' status — 25 TJ/day from 2027; represents a potential new domestic supply source but is not yet committed

key_quotes.speaker: AEMO
key_quotes.quote: All scenarios identify the need for new supply investments to maintain supply adequacy. Based on current forecasts, a combination of solutions will be required in the long term.
key_quotes.context: 2025 GSOO executive summary on east coast gas supply adequacy

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Pre-war official forecast showing domestic gas supply already tightening from 2028; combined with Qatar LNG infrastructure destruction (decade-long), creates a domestic supply squeeze vs. export demand surge tension that Australian policy has no framework to manage
sources.notes: Published March 2025 — predates the Iran war by 12 months. The pre-war domestic supply gap forecast makes the war's LNG demand surge even more acute. AEMO is the official market operator; this is primary source government data.

sources.source_name: Australian Government — Department of Industry, Science and Resources
sources.author: Hon Madeleine King MP, Minister for Resources
sources.title: Future Gas Strategy

claims.text: The Australian Government explicitly commits to meeting long-term LNG export contracts: 'Australia is committed to meeting our long-term contracts'
Gas is needed through to 2050 and beyond under all net zero scenarios — it is a 'transition fuel' that firms renewable power and supports manufacturing until alternatives are viable

The strategy's objectives include: safeguard energy security and affordability; entrench Australia's reputation as an attractive trade and investment destination; help trade partners on their own paths to net zero

Australia's LNG export customers — primarily Japan, South Korea, China and Taiwan — are relying on Australian gas to transition their economies to net zero; all are now facing acute energy stress from the Iran war

The Future Gas Strategy was developed entirely within a net zero transition framework — it contains no scenario analysis for conflict-driven supply disruption; the 'energy security' objective is framed around market stability, not war
Continued investment in gas supply and transport infrastructure is needed — 'we cannot rely on past investments to get us through the next decades'; new development required

key_quotes.speaker: Hon Madeleine King MP
key_quotes.quote: Australia is and will remain a reliable and trusted trade and investment partner, including for LNG. Our trade partners have made large investments over decades in Australia's resources industry. They are relying on Australian gas to transition their economies to net zero.
key_quotes.context: Minister's foreword to Future Gas Strategy

sources.relevance_to_assessment: Official government policy framework calibrated for orderly net zero transition — not for conflict scenarios. The strategy's explicit commitment to LNG export contracts creates a policy tension: domestic supply tightening from 2028 vs. export obligations to LNG customers now desperate due to Qatar/South Pars destruction
sources.notes: Primary source — official government policy document. The absence of conflict scenarios in the strategy is itself analytically significant: it demonstrates the 'fair-weather' approach the Lowy 'We Told You So' article criticised.
